title: Address Injection Followed by High-Value Rush Purchase Pattern
narrative: Attacker gains account credentials and immediately adds a new shipping address (dist1 > 500km from historical addr1), then places a single high-value order ($800-$2,500) within 15-45 minutes to that fresh address. The legitimate account typically shows 6-18 months of purchase history with consistent delivery locations (addr1 entropy < 0.3) and average order values $45-$120. The fraudulent transaction occurs during off-hours (txn_hour between 01:00-05:00 local time) when legitimate user is typically inactive. Product selection shifts dramatically to high-resale electronics (ProductCD = 'C' or 'H') versus historical clothing/household purchases. Card verification passes (CVV match, AVS partial) because attacker uses saved payment method, but session features show new device fingerprint and IP geolocation mismatch of 800+ miles from P_emaildomain registration country.
indicators: New R_emaildomain or addr1 added to account within 60 minutes before transaction
dist1 value exceeds 500km from historical median delivery location
TransactionAmt 4.5x-15x higher than card1_amt_mean from prior 90 days
Transaction occurs during txn_hour outside user's historical 95th percentile activity window
Device fingerprint (DeviceInfo hash) appears for first time on account
ProductCD category shift from historical modal category
Time delta between address addition and purchase < 45 minutes
summary: A previously consistent account suddenly adds a new shipping address over 500km away, then within 15-45 minutes places a single order worth 4.5-15x their normal spending during unusual hours (1-5 AM), shipping high-resale electronics to the new address from a device never seen before on the account.
reasoning: Criminals who steal credentials want to monetize quickly before the victim notices, so they rush a high-value order to an address they control, targeting resellable goods like electronics that can be fenced for cash — the speed, distance, value spike, and device change together reveal someone other than the account owner is making the purchase.
indicator_explanations: Account's email or shipping address was just changed moments before purchase
Delivery destination is hundreds of miles from where this user normally ships
Purchase amount is vastly larger than this card's typical spending pattern
Transaction happens at an unusual hour for this account's activity rhythm
Account is suddenly being accessed from a completely new device
Purchase category doesn't match what this user normally buys
Attacker moved fast from hijacking account to rushing through expensive order
